name: match_real4_syscollector
description: Mirrors a real-world lab of 4 Wazuh agents with ONLY syscollector enabled (fim, sca, agent-info disabled). Each agent opens 1 dominant session on the manager — 4 sessions total — and the load is the syscollector first-sync of a Linux box with ~2000 installed packages. Used as the bench-side comparison point against a real 4-agent run so RSS / workers_q / sessions can be compared 1:1.
notes: Why agents=4: only syscollector is enabled in the real lab. The manager sees one session per agent for syscollector. Note that internally syscollector also spawns a syscollector_vd sync protocol, but its DataValue volume is negligible compared to the package inventory — safe to ignore for this comparison.

Why sessions_per_agent=1: a real Wazuh agent emits exactly ONE first-sync after scan_on_start, then sits idle until the next <interval> (1h default). Without this cap the bench would loop the session inside the duration_sec window (~10-20 sessions per agent in 600s), which is NOT what the real lab does. With sessions_per_agent=1 each bench agent runs Start → 2000 DataValue → End → exit, matching real behaviour 1:1.

Why duration_sec=180: just a safety ceiling. With max_eps=75 and 2000 DataValues, each session takes ~27 s; 180 s is plenty to let all 4 agents finish their single session plus the drain window. The sender exits cleanly the moment every agent has completed its one session.

Why data_size=2000: typical syscollector first-sync of a desktop Linux box has ~1500-3000 packages. After warm-up the deltas are much smaller; this scenario emulates the moment-of-stress (manager restart, fresh agent join).
Why payload_kind=package (no payload_size override): matches what real syscollector emits. Average ~400-800 bytes per DataValue.

Why use_databatch=true + batch_size=50: the real Wazuh 5 agent groups DataValues into DataBatch messages (~50 items per wire frame) instead of sending one DataValue per message. Without this flag the bench sends 2002 individual messages per session and inflates RSS far more than the real agent does. With it the bench matches the real agent 1:1 on wire shape, throughput and memory pressure.

Compared with the real 4-agent run, this scenario should produce a similar workers_q peak, sessions ≈ 4, and a similar RSS slope IF the slow_workers patch is in the binary. Without the patch, both should drain healthily.

How to compare: run this scenario, then run the real 4-agent test capturing monitor+log_parser on the manager (engine/tools/devContainer/scripts/monitor.py + log_parser.py). Then: ./run_benchmark.sh --compare results_match_real4_syscollector results_real_agents_4
